items.q: Does choosing a faster tempo show greater skill in a performance exam?
items.correct: No - the tempo must serve the music; a controlled, stylish performance at a sensible speed scores better than a rushed, inaccurate one.
items.misconception: Playing the piece faster than marked impresses examiners.
items.random_wrong: Examiners award extra marks for finishing early.
items.why: Speed beyond your secure control damages accuracy, fluency and style, all of which carry marks; tempo choices should match the composer's intention and your technique.

items.q: Should you stop and correct yourself when you make an error in the exam performance?
items.correct: No - keep going and maintain the pulse; fluency and overall shape matter more than one slip.
items.misconception: Stopping to fix a wrong note shows the examiner you know the correct version.
items.random_wrong: You should restart the whole programme after any mistake.
items.why: Stopping magnifies a small error into a major break in continuity; performers are trained to recover seamlessly because assessment rewards an unbroken musical line.

items.q: Are the dynamics printed in the score optional extras?
items.correct: No - realising dynamics and other expressive markings is part of an accurate, stylish performance and is directly assessed.
items.misconception: Dynamics can be ignored as long as the notes and rhythms are right.
items.why: Notation specifies more than pitch and rhythm; flattening out the dynamics removes the music's character and loses interpretation marks even when every note is correct.

items.correct: Maintaining continuity and a steady pulse
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Maintaining continuity and a steady pulse. “Playing as fast as possible” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which is the best way to manage performance nerves?
items.misconception: Avoid practising the piece in the final week
items.random_wrong: Choosing a piece far above your level
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Thorough preparation including mock performances. “Avoid practising the piece in the final week” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Phrasing in a melody is best described as what?
items.correct: Shaping the line into sentence-like units with direction
items.misconception: Playing every note at equal strength
items.random_wrong: Adding extra notes to the score
items.why: It's Shaping the line into sentence-like units with direction. “Playing every note at equal strength” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Practising with a metronome chiefly develops what?
items.correct: A steady pulse and rhythmic accuracy
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A steady pulse and rhythmic accuracy. “Better tone colour” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
